title: Colour planning for weavers
subtitle: How warp and weft colours interact in woven cloth, and how to predict the result before warping
excerpt: Woven cloth mixes colour optically: at each intersection, either the warp or the weft is visible on the surface, and the eye blends those alternating threads from a distance. The overall colour depends on the weave structure, the sett, and which colour appears more often on the face of the cloth. This reading covers warp-dominant and weft-dominant structures, optical blending in tabby, and a simple card-wrap method for testing colour combinations before committing a full warp.
sourceNotes: Colour interaction in woven cloth is a well-documented property of weave structure. Principles here are consistent with Albers' work on colour and with contemporary guild instruction on colour planning.

content.text: At each intersection in a woven cloth, either the warp or the weft lies on top. The eye blends the two colours that alternate across the surface, producing
. How much of each colour appears depends on the weave structure: in tabby, they appear equally; in a
such as 3/1 twill, the warp colour dominates.

In a balanced tabby cloth, warp and weft contribute equally to the surface. A yellow warp and a blue weft produce a cloth that reads as greenish-yellow at a normal viewing distance, because the alternating yellow and blue threads blend in the eye. The result is softer and more luminous than a physical paint mix of the same two colours, because each thread retains its own colour at close range.

Change the weave structure and the visible colour proportion changes. In a 3/1 twill, the warp passes over three weft threads for every one it passes under. The warp colour therefore dominates the face, and the weft colour appears only faintly in the gaps between floats. Weaving the same warp and weft in tabby versus 3/1 twill produces noticeably different apparent colours from identical yarns. A pale weft can shift the hue of a strong warp without replacing it.

A denser sett (more ends per cm) produces more warp coverage, making the warp colour more dominant even in tabby. A looser sett lets more weft show. For the most balanced blend in tabby, aim for a square sett: roughly the same number of weft picks per cm as warp ends per cm. If the weft is beaten in more loosely, the warp colour will dominate; more firmly, and the weft colour gains ground.

Before warping a large project, make a colour card. Wind each candidate warp colour across a stiff card at the planned sett, leaving alternate gaps between the threads. Thread a contrasting weft colour through the gaps with a needle and compare the apparent blend. The card shows the approximate surface colour at your sett without committing a full warp. A small card takes 10 minutes; a mismatched warp costs hours.

When planning warp stripes, the width of each stripe relative to the sett determines how distinct it will appear. A stripe of 4 ends at 8 ends per cm is 5 mm wide and clearly visible. A stripe of 2 ends is only 2.5 mm and may blend optically into the adjacent colour, particularly in fine yarn. For weft-face stripes (changing the weft colour between rows), any stripe wider than 4 picks will register as a distinct band; narrower stripes blend into each other and produce a gradated transition.
